evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go defines both protocol-level C2C actions and upload-service actions in one catalog file (ProtocolActionSpecs, UploadActionSpecs, AllActionSpecs), creating a shared hub across unrelated ownership boundaries.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go enforces canonical binding parity by traversing fixtures and canonical bindings, indicating boundary correctness relies on cross-package test choreography rather than narrower package ownership seams.

goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go states canonical policy lives in protocol/contracts/conformance and architecture docs, which confirms governance is coordinated across multiple modules rather than encapsulated behind a single stable boundary.

goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go mixes domain capabilities (C2C and upload) and ownership constants in one file, so top-level decomposition reflects historical aggregation rather than capability-isolated modules.

goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go encodes critical governance expectations in comments that point to tests/docs elsewhere, while goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go validates only parser accept/reject behavior; this split makes package responsibility less self-explanatory.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go contains broad catalog-governance assertions for upload and startram, concentrating cross-domain policy checks in a single conformance test surface.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows very large top-level packages for this batch scope: goseg/shipworkflow/ has 55 files, goseg/config/ has 41 files, and goseg/docker/orchestration/ has 34 files (all well above the >10 mixed-concern split threshold in the packet guidance).

Assigned files in goseg/shipworkflow mix transport/message handling (HandleNewShip), input normalization/validation (normalizePatp/isValidPatp), async lifecycle execution (go ProvisionShip), and runtime dependency wiring (newShipProvisioningRuntime) in one directory/package.

Assigned files in goseg/docker/orchestration mix container-type dispatch (container_config.go) and runtime adapter/bridge assembly (container_bridge.go) in the same root orchestration directory that also holds operational runtime logic.
`goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` validates action token parsing only (known/unknown actions) and does not validate the payload-rule contract described in `goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go`.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` focuses on fixture/catalog consistency and payload rule metadata exposure, but does not assert upload runtime behavior against those payload rules in this batch.

README declares contract conformance as a backend quality gate, but these listed tests leave a gap between declared upload payload policy and direct runtime verification in the uploadsvc test surface.

goseg/shipworkflow/doc.go states a stable transition contract (loading -> success or error), but goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go emits both "freeError" and "error" event types for failure/reset paths.

goseg/shipworkflow/newship_service.go HandleNewShipBoot returns nil after spawning a goroutine, so downstream ProvisionShip failures are not represented in the function return contract even though ProvisionShip itself is error-returning in goseg/shipworkflow/newship.go.

goseg/docker/orchestration/container_config.go documents ContainerConfigForTypeString as parsing external input, but implementation only type-casts string to transition.ContainerType and defers failure to later map lookup.

findings.summary: A single action catalog file acts as a cross-domain hub, coupling upload-service and protocol C2C boundaries.
evidence: ProtocolActionSpecs() (C2C) and UploadActionSpecs() (uploadsvc) are co-located in goseg/protocol/contracts/catalog/action/specs.go and merged via AllActionSpecs().
The file carries multiple ownership contexts (OwnerSystemWiFi and OwnerUploadService), showing boundary aggregation in one module.
Conformance tests in goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go must verify these mappings globally, which is consistent with a central coupling hub.
findings.suggestion: Split catalog definitions by capability/owner (for example, separate upload and c2c spec providers) and compose through a thin registry interface so each domain owns its own contract set with minimized cross-module blast radius.

findings.summary: Contract-governance responsibility is fragmented across comments, protocol conformance tests, and local parser tests, reducing top-level ownership clarity.
evidence: goseg/uploadsvc/doc.go declares strict action-contract policy but points enforcement to external conformance and architecture docs.
goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go only tests ParseUploadAction accept/reject paths and does not assert the documented payload-rule contract boundary.

goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go carries cross-domain assertions (upload bindings, payload rules, startram catalog drift), centralizing policy checks away from feature-local ownership.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single authoritative contract-governance seam (e.g., package-level contract validator API consumed by uploadsvc and conformance tests) and keep feature-local tests asserting policy behavior through that seam to make ownership and decomposition explicit.

findings.summary: Core directories are oversized and mix distinct concerns, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: Directory profile evidence in packet: shipworkflow (55 files), config (41), orchestration (34), each above the >10-file mixed-concern warning threshold.
Within assigned files, responsibilities span message transport parsing, workflow orchestration, runtime composition, and container runtime adaptation without subpackage isolation.
The layout makes change paths non-local (a single feature touch requires navigating multiple concern types in the same directory roots).
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 create `goseg/shipworkflow/entrypoints` and move `newship_service.go` there, keep `newship.go` in workflow core; Stage 2 create `goseg/docker/orchestration/adapters` and move `container_bridge.go` plus `container_config.go` there (or split into `adapters/runtime` and `adapters/config`); Stage 3 create `goseg/config/bootstrap` for startup-only logic and keep `goseg/config` as state/query API; Stage 4 update imports with `goimports ./...` and targeted `git mv`; Stage 5 validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/... ./goseg/docker/orchestration/... ./goseg/config/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Container runtime bridge/config-dispatch code is placed in root orchestration instead of a dedicated adapter boundary.
evidence: container_bridge.go is a pure adapter/composition layer converting dockerRuntime to container runtime structs and seam merges.
container_config.go is a type-dispatch map from transition.ContainerType to builder functions and delegates to bridge-produced runtimes.
Both files are adapter concerns but sit in root orchestration package, increasing directory-level concern mixing and making boundaries less discoverable.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 add `goseg/docker/orchestration/adapters/containercfg`; Stage 2 move `container_bridge.go` and `container_config.go` (and test) into that folder; Stage 3 keep a thin forwarding API in root orchestration only if external imports require stability; Stage 4 run `go test ./goseg/docker/orchestration/...` and `go test ./...` after import updates.

findings.summary: New ship entrypoint combines transport, validation, domain events, and async orchestration in one module.
evidence: HandleNewShip decodes JSON payloads and routes action commands; HandleNewShipBoot then performs key decoding, drive lifecycle preparation, patp validation, event emission, and goroutine launch.
newShipProvisioningRuntime wires many external dependencies directly, making the same package both entrypoint and composition root.
doc.go describes broad lifecycle orchestration, but concrete files include additional transport-level and validation concerns beyond a focused workflow boundary.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 extract payload decode/route into `goseg/shipworkflow/entrypoints/newship_ws.go`; Stage 2 extract validation/key normalization into `goseg/shipworkflow/validation/newship_validation.go`; Stage 3 isolate runtime wiring in `goseg/shipworkflow/composition/newship_runtime.go`; Stage 4 keep `shipworkflow` root as domain workflow API only; Stage 5 update imports and validate with `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Config package surface mixes bootstrap lifecycle, mutable process context, and snapshot DTOs, weakening top-level role clarity.
evidence: bootstrap.go owns initialization sequencing, persistence bootstrapping, and runtime path/debug initialization.
config.go defines runtime context aliases/accessors plus multiple domain settings snapshot structs and startram state accessors.
Callers cannot easily distinguish bootstrap-only APIs from steady-state config APIs by package boundary alone.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 introduce `goseg/config/bootstrap` (init lifecycle) and `goseg/config/runtime` (context accessors); Stage 2 keep `goseg/config` as stable facade with narrow exported API; Stage 3 move DTO snapshot types into `goseg/config/snapshots` if still shared; Stage 4 run `go test ./goseg/config/...` and `go test ./...` after import rewrites (`goimports ./...`).

findings.summary: Contract governance has no single source of truth, forcing manual synchronization across docs, catalog constants, and conformance tests.
evidence: README defines backend contract gates and references governance docs, while `uploadsvc/doc.go` independently states payload policy constraints.
Catalog files encode contract IDs/names/messages as literals, and conformance tests rebuild expected state and compare against canonical catalog entries.
No machine-readable governance manifest is referenced by both docs and tests in these files; consistency depends on distributed updates.
findings.suggestion: Create a canonical governance manifest under `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/` (owners, payload rules, quality-gate invariants), derive catalog specs and conformance fixtures from it, and generate a short policy excerpt consumed by docs. Keep conformance tests focused on manifest-to-runtime projection correctness rather than independent literal duplication.

findings.summary: Family contract spec declarations repeat bespoke patterns instead of using one domain-level declaration shape.
evidence: Action and StarTram families each hand-roll constant sets and constructor functions with similar semantic fields (ID/Name/Description/Owner/Message).
Conformance tests must carry family-specific translation logic to assert equivalence, increasing policy ceremony as families grow.
The current approach is correct but structurally repetitive and raises the cost of adding new contract families.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared family declaration schema (for example `familyspec.Declaration`) and per-family thin adapters, then refactor tests to consume the same declaration form. This keeps ownership and contract shape explicit while reducing repeated bespoke family wiring.

findings.summary: Upload service tests cover action parsing but do not directly test the documented payload-rule behavior.
evidence: `actions_test.go` validates only known/unknown action parsing outcomes.
`doc.go` states strict payload contract requirements (reset forbids open-endpoint payload; open-endpoint requires endpoint/token).
`fixtures_test.go` validates payload-rule metadata consistency, but this file set does not include tests asserting uploadsvc runtime behavior against those rules.
findings.suggestion: Add table-driven uploadsvc runtime tests that exercise payload validation paths (`reset` with forbidden fields, `open-endpoint` missing required fields, valid payload success) and wire them into the documented backend contract gate command so docs and executable guarantees stay aligned.

findings.summary: Contract governance concern is split across root docs, runtime package docs, and multiple protocol subdirectories, reducing navigability.
evidence: Directory profiles show governance logic partitioned across several small protocol/contracts directories, while key policy text lives outside that tree (`README.md`, `uploadsvc/doc.go`).
Readers must traverse root docs + runtime docs + protocol catalog + conformance tests to understand one contract policy area.
This placement pattern weakens locality of change for governance updates.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/` for policy-facing docs and generated policy summaries; (2) move contract-family declarations under `goseg/protocol/contracts/families/<family>/` (move order: startram then action, then shared helpers) while keeping compatibility aliases temporarily; (3) colocate conformance specs under `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/conformance/`; (4) update imports with `rg "protocol/contracts/catalog|protocol/contracts/conformance|uploadsvc/doc" -n` then `go test ./protocol/contracts/... ./uploadsvc/...`; (5) update README references to the new governance entrypoint and re-run `go test ./...` to validate no broken paths.

findings.summary: HandleNewShipBoot advertises synchronous error signaling but suppresses provisioning failures behind async event side effects.
evidence: HandleNewShipBoot returns error but starts ProvisionShip in a goroutine and immediately returns nil on launch success.
ProvisionShip returns error in newship.go, but async failures are only sent through PublishNewShipTransition via handleError, not the function return path.
findings.suggestion: Make the contract explicit and single-path: either (a) return immediately with an async job handle and no error-return promise, or (b) execute provisioning synchronously and return real failures; update callers and docs to match.

findings.summary: Ship workflow failure/reset events use inconsistent error event keys, weakening the observable transition contract.
evidence: Package contract in doc.go describes stable phase transitions ending in success or error.
HandleNewShipBoot publishes transition type "freeError" for failures, while ResetNewShip clears transition type "error", creating two parallel error channels.
findings.suggestion: Define a single canonical error transition type constant for new-ship workflow and use it consistently for emit/clear paths; add tests that assert transition type vocabulary and ordering.

findings.summary: ContainerConfigForTypeString documentation claims parsing semantics that the function does not implement.
evidence: Comment says "parses external string input and delegates to the typed API".
Implementation performs direct cast transition.ContainerType(containerType) and relies on later lookup failure, without normalization/validation that "parse" implies.
findings.suggestion: Align behavior and contract by either implementing explicit parsing/normalization with typed errors, or rewriting the comment and external docs to state this is a thin cast+dispatch helper.
